Here's a one-page PRD outline for dark mode.

# PRD: Dark Mode Theme

**Author:** [name] · **Status:** Draft · **Last updated:** 2026-06-22

## 1. Summary
Add a user-selectable dark theme to the web app, with a light/dark/system toggle that persists across sessions and respects the OS preference by default.

## 2. Problem & Motivation
- Users in low-light environments report eye strain on the current light-only UI.
- Dark mode is a baseline expectation for modern web apps; its absence shows up in support requests and reviews.
- Competitors ship it; we're at parity risk.

## 3. Goals
- Users can switch between Light, Dark, and System themes.
- Selection persists across sessions and devices (where logged in).
- All primary screens render correctly in dark mode with accessible contrast.

## 4. Non-Goals
- Custom/user-defined color themes or per-component theming.
- Theming of email templates, marketing site, or third-party embeds.
- A full design-system overhaul beyond what dark mode requires.

## 5. Users & Use Cases
- Night/low-light usage on desktop and mobile web.
- Users whose OS is already set to dark and expect the app to follow.

## 6. Requirements

**Functional**
- Theme toggle in settings (and optionally a quick-access control in the top nav).
- Three modes: Light, Dark, System (follows `prefers-color-scheme`).
- Default = System on first visit; no flash of wrong theme on load.
- Preference saved to user profile (logged-in) and local storage (anonymous).

**Non-functional**
- WCAG AA contrast (≥4.5:1 text) in both themes.
- Theme switch is instant, no full page reload.
- No measurable impact on page-load performance.

## 7. Design & Technical Approach
- Define a semantic color-token layer (e.g. `--surface`, `--text-primary`) mapped per theme rather than hard-coded colors.
- Apply theme via a root-level attribute/class; read OS preference via media query.
- Audit existing components for hard-coded colors, shadows, and images that assume a light background.

## 8. Success Metrics
- % of active users who enable Dark or System within 30 days of launch.
- No regression in accessibility audit scores.
- Reduction in eye-strain / dark-mode-related support tickets.

## 9. Rollout
- Internal dogfood → staged rollout behind a feature flag → GA.
- Track theme-toggle events; monitor contrast/visual-bug reports.

## 10. Open Questions
- Sync theme across devices, or keep per-device?
- Ship a nav-bar quick toggle in v1, or settings-only?
- Scope of legacy screens/components that need remediation before launch?

---

Two decisions worth settling before this leaves draft: cross-device sync vs. per-device, and whether the v1 toggle lives in the nav or only in settings. Want me to save this as a markdown file, or fill in any section in more depth?

# PRD: Dark Mode Theme

**Author:** [name] · **Status:** Draft · **Last updated:** 2026-06-22

## 1. Summary
Add a user-selectable dark theme to the web app, with a light/dark/system toggle that persists across sessions and respects the OS preference by default.

## 2. Problem & Motivation
- Users in low-light environments report eye strain on the current light-only UI.
- Dark mode is a baseline expectation for modern web apps; its absence shows up in support requests and reviews.
- Competitors ship it; we're at parity risk.

## 3. Goals
- Users can switch between Light, Dark, and System themes.
- Selection persists across sessions and devices (where logged in).
- All primary screens render correctly in dark mode with accessible contrast.

## 4. Non-Goals
- Custom/user-defined color themes or per-component theming.
- Theming of email templates, marketing site, or third-party embeds.
- A full design-system overhaul beyond what dark mode requires.

## 5. Users & Use Cases
- Night/low-light usage on desktop and mobile web.
- Users whose OS is already set to dark and expect the app to follow.

## 6. Requirements

**Functional**
- Theme toggle in settings (and optionally a quick-access control in the top nav).
- Three modes: Light, Dark, System (follows `prefers-color-scheme`).
- Default = System on first visit; no flash of wrong theme on load.
- Preference saved to user profile (logged-in) and local storage (anonymous).

**Non-functional**
- WCAG AA contrast (≥4.5:1 text) in both themes.
- Theme switch is instant, no full page reload.
- No measurable impact on page-load performance.

## 7. Design & Technical Approach
- Define a semantic color-token layer (e.g. `--surface`, `--text-primary`) mapped per theme rather than hard-coded colors.
- Apply theme via a root-level attribute/class; read OS preference via media query.
- Audit existing components for hard-coded colors, shadows, and images that assume a light background.

## 8. Success Metrics
- % of active users who enable Dark or System within 30 days of launch.
- No regression in accessibility audit scores.
- Reduction in eye-strain / dark-mode-related support tickets.

## 9. Rollout
- Internal dogfood → staged rollout behind a feature flag → GA.
- Track theme-toggle events; monitor contrast/visual-bug reports.

## 10. Recommendation
**Recommend shipping v1 as: System default + Light/Dark/System toggle, with the toggle in settings only, and theme synced across devices for logged-in users.** This is the lowest-risk path to parity: System-default matches user expectation with no onboarding, settings-only keeps the nav uncluttered while we validate adoption, and profile-based sync avoids the "dark on laptop, light on phone" inconsistency that drives support tickets. A nav quick-toggle and any legacy-screen remediation beyond the primary flows are deferred to a fast-follow once adoption data is in.

## 11. Open Questions (resolved recommendations in **bold**; open for veto)
- Sync theme across devices, or keep per-device? — **Recommend cross-device sync for logged-in users** (profile-stored), per-device fallback for anonymous.
- Ship a nav-bar quick toggle in v1, or settings-only? — **Recommend settings-only for v1**, revisit nav toggle post-launch based on usage.
- Scope of legacy screens/components that need remediation before launch? — **Recommend scoping v1 to the primary flows only**; pending an audit to confirm the legacy list, treat non-primary screens as fast-follow.

---

My recommendation for what to settle before this leaves draft is above: ship System-default with a settings-only toggle and cross-device sync for logged-in users, deferring the nav quick-toggle and non-primary legacy screens to a fast-follow. The two calls most worth your explicit sign-off (or veto) are cross-device sync and the settings-only toggle. Want me to save this as a markdown file, or fill in any section in more depth?
